segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic, fast-paced montage of a man in a black t-shirt running through a dimly lit, industrial-looking space filled with smoke and debris, suggesting urgency. This quickly cuts to actor Ben McKenzie, in a dark blue button-up shirt, speaking earnestly in a softly lit interview setting, criticizing a viral crypto promotion as having gone viral "not in a good way" and calling crypto an "easy paycheck." A dramatic space view of Mars, glowing orange against a starry black void, then appears, prominently featuring the 'crypto.com' logo, accompanied by a deep, cinematic voiceover stating, "Fortune favors the brave." The scene returns to Ben McKenzie, who continues to speak with expressive facial movements, explaining how "easy it is to get lured in to cryptocurrency," especially when you see "all of your friends and neighbors... getting rich." The video then cuts to freelance reporter Jacob Silverman, in a light gray button-up shirt, speaking with a calm and engaged expression in a similar softly lit interview setting, questioning how the Fed figured into crypto's rise and if "so much money sloshing around" just needed to go somewhere. McKenzie briefly interjects with a confirming "Yeah." Silverman continues, stating that "when money is cheap, people gamble," and that "fraud runs rampant," adding that even "within crypto circles, people start talking about Ponzi schemes... in a non-derisive way," as the camera alternates between close-ups of their engaged expressions, with the 'FRONTLINE' lower-third identifying the segment.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a dynamic, fast-paced montage featuring a man in a black t-shirt running through a dimly lit, industrial-looking space with smoke and debris, suggesting urgency or escape. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a modern, warmly lit interview room featuring exposed brick and large windows, where three men are seated around a glass table. James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard and dark blue button-up shirt, is seen holding papers and listening intently as Jim Chanos, an older man with white hair in a dark suit and blue tie, speaks with a serious expression about "irrational thinking and rationalization" creating an "illusion that there's value here until something pops it." The camera cuts to a close-up of Jacoby, who animatedly asks about "speed," before a narrator (Jacoby's voice) introduces Jim Chanos, founder of Kynikos Associates, as a serious investor speaking out. Chanos then describes the period as an "orgy of speculation" by mid-2021, where "anyone who wanted to raise money for anything could do so." He expresses concern over "the amount of fraud we saw being floated on top of legitimate companies," particularly in the unregulated "crypto space" with "new coins or NFTs" being sold to a public eager for the "latest fad," adding, "And that, that bothered me." Jacoby, holding a pen to his lips in thought, then directly asks if Chanos would "draw a direct link between what the Fed was doing and the crypto craze," to which Chanos responds that "it was all part of speculation," as the camera alternates between their engaged close-ups under soft, natural light, with their names and titles displayed on-screen, including the FRONTLINE logo.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a bald man in a dark suit and red tie, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures and wide eyes, arguing against raising interest rates to control crypto like Bitcoin if it means putting millions of Americans out of work, calling it a "bad trade." A distinct "whoosh" sound accompanies the transition to a black screen with white text reading 'CHAPTER THIRTEEN' and 'Economics 101'. Serious, investigative background music begins as the narrative shifts to exterior shots of prominent Washington D.C. landmarks at dusk and night. The Federal Reserve Building is shown with its classical architecture and illuminated windows, while a male voice states that "interest rates and inflation are gonna rise well above what the Fed has projected," and another male voice adds that "as the markets were heating up, so were concerns that the Fed's policies would fuel inflation." A time-lapse of the Washington Monument with streaks of car lights follows, as a female voice reports that "prices are rising at the fastest pace in more than a decade." The U.S. Treasury Building with a statue is then seen, while a male voice notes, "But it wasn't just what the Fed was doing," and another male voice, sounding like a quote, declares, "I'm going to help the American people who are hurting now." The White House, brightly lit against the dark sky with a full moon, is shown as a speaker explains, "The new Biden administration was sending $1400 checks to many Americans." A female voice adds that "stimulus money from the latest COVID relief bill is arriving in bank accounts all over the country," and a final male voice mentions "extending unemployment benefits, tax credits, and other" measures. The sequence concludes with a sweeping aerial night view of a river and highway interchange, with light trails from moving vehicles and reflections on the water, capturing the city’s infrastructure and movement under a dark, cloudy sky, as the economic discussion continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial night-time view of Washington D.C., showcasing illuminated highways, the Washington Monument, and the U.S. Capitol Building under a deep blue sky. The scene transitions to Lawrence H. Summers, identified as a Wall Street Week contributor, speaking directly to the camera in his well-lit home office, with the on-screen text 'STIMULUS SUGAR HIGH?'. He states, "I think there's a real possibility that within the year, we're going to be dealing with the most serious incipient inflation problem that we have faced in the last 40 years." This is followed by black-and-white archival footage of Summers speaking at a formal event, as a narrator explains that "critics like former Treasury Secretary Larry Summers were publicly expressing concern that all the stimulus money from the Fed and the government would boost economic demand at a time when supply problems from the pandemic were still an issue." The video then cuts to Nouriel Roubini, labeled as an economist in a FRONTLINE segment, speaking in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls. Roubini, with expressive hand movements, asserts, "People like myself, like Larry Summers and others, saw that that massive stimulus, that was unprecedented, an order of magnitude greater than the one we had after the global financial crisis, will lead to excessive demand, overheating, and inflation." A bald man with a beard is briefly seen listening intently in a warmly lit room with string lights before Roubini continues, detailing, "So we had an unprecedented fiscal stimulus, an unprecedented monetary stimulus. We had bailout checks sent to everybody, every household, every firm, every financial institution." The sequence maintains a professional, analytical tone, discussing economic policy and stimulus effects.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Dion Rabouin, a Black man with braided hair, rectangular glasses, a light blue dress shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit indoor setting. He gestures animatedly as he explains that "too much" money was pushed into the economy, leading to price increases, stating, "There really just was all this money being pushed out in the economy. At the same time, you've got the Federal Reserve, they're pushing out another four or five trillion dollars into the economy. And so prices rose." The scene then transitions to an aerial view of a large parking lot, followed by high-angle shots of a well-lit grocery store aisle and a warehouse-style store with shoppers. A cashier scans items at a checkout, then a man in a black hoodie walks through a bustling multi-level shopping mall. A news broadcast from NBC News appears with a lower-third graphic 'THE PRICE YOU PAY' and 'CONSUMER PRICES SURGE TO NEARLY 13-YEAR HIGH', showing a man in a black face mask outdoors, while a male anchor reports, "Core CPI inflation is set to rise sharply over the next three months." Dion Rabouin's voice returns, explaining, "This goes back to your economics 101 textbook, right? When there's too much money chasing too few goods, prices go up and that drives inflation higher." The visuals continue with a busy city street, a top-down view of a grocery store, a wide shot of a multi-level shopping mall, and people walking through a mall corridor. A male news anchor states, "It only took a few months for the warnings to come true," followed by a female anchor adding, "It seems like everything across the board is becoming more expensive." Another male voice reports, "Gas prices going up, food prices going up," as close-ups of onions and other produce at an outdoor market are shown, then people walking on a city sidewalk. A male news anchor calmly notes, "But the Fed didn't flinch," and another urgently adds, "A surge in energy, housing, and food costs." The calm anchor continues, "It didn't raise interest rates or pull back on quantitative easing." President Joe Biden is seen speaking at a podium in a formal chamber, followed by Jerome Powell seated at a formal hearing table. A male news anchor dramatically poses, "The question now haunting economists is whether these price hikes are a pandemic blip or a sign of a long-term threat to the economy," and another reports, "And they had a word for the highest inflation in more than a decade: transitory." Multiple voices rapidly repeat "Transitory" as the video concludes with a man in a dark suit and glasses, speaking in a modern, dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, calmly stating, "Now, I know you believe this is transitory, but everything's transitory."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide, high-angle shot of a modern, multi-level shopping mall, followed by an aerial view of a sprawling car dealership complex, establishing a commercial and urban environment, all underscored by a subtle, almost cinematic background sound. The scene quickly transitions to a live Bloomberg news segment featuring Mohamed El-Erian, identified as 'Queens' College, Cambridge President,' speaking in front of a bookshelf backdrop, with on-screen text reading 'EL-ERIAN: INFLATION ROUND NOT TRANSITORY.' El-Erian's voice is heard emphatically stating, "This inflation round is not transitory. This is a very hot inflation environment, and the longer the central banks wait, the greater the risk." The video then cuts to a more intimate interview setting, where El-Erian, now identified as 'Chief economic advisor, Allianz,' continues his argument with expressive gestures and a serious demeanor, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, in a dimly lit room with large windows. He explains his strong reaction to the "transitory" assertion, noting, "I remember warning at that time that we simply don't have enough evidence that it is going to be transitory. Transitory is a very reassuring term... Therefore we don't need to change behavior." The camera alternates between El-Erian and a bald, bearded interviewer in a dark blue button-up shirt, who listens intently, occasionally nodding. The interviewer's voice then asks, "What kind of evidence were you seeing this may be stickier inflation than it is transitory?" El-Erian responds, citing, "One, what companies were telling us. And companies were saying, I am not so sure it's transitory. This is beyond the pandemic. I was talking to CEOs. And they were giving me a very clear message." The FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower third during the interview, maintaining a professional, analytical tone, with close-ups capturing the speakers' facial expressions and the subtle dynamics of the conversation, underscoring the importance of the economic insights being shared.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, speaking with expressive hand gestures and wide eyes, emphasizing that they "not view the disruptions as being transitory." The FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower third. The camera then cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard in a dark blue shirt, who listens intently, holding a pen to his lips in a thoughtful pose, asking, "Why transitory? Why that word, what did you think at the time?" Kashkari, back in close-up, continues with emphatic gestures, explaining, "Well, we saw a number of factors that we thought were conspiring to lead to high prices and that many of those factors would fade away over time. So, for example, uh supply chains we saw were getting gummed up. But we also know that businesses were working very hard to un-ungum those up, to untangle those supply chains. So we thought that they'd probably make more progress there than we expected." Jacoby, still listening thoughtfully with his pen to his lips, then poses another question, referencing the "business round table" and their CEOs seeing "inflation everywhere," asking, "How does something like that land for you at that time?" Kashkari responds, maintaining his expressive demeanor, stating, "I mean, I take it seriously. I I don't dismiss it. Then I I map it against the data that we're seeing. But I'll just say if we did not have an outlier view on inflation or the economy overall. If you look at the consensus of forecasts among experts in America, on Wall Street, around the world, they all basically had the same forecast, which is inflation is going to be transitory. It's" The professional, well-lit setting with natural light remains consistent throughout the dynamic exchange.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, speaking with expressive hand gestures and wide eyes, indicating emphasis and urgency. The setting is a modern, well-lit interior with large windows and soft natural light, suggesting a professional office or interview space. The camera alternates between close-ups of Kashkari and a second man, identified as Correspondent James Jacoby, who listens intently, holding a pen to his lips in a thoughtful pose. Jacoby is bald with a beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, and is seated in a modern room with large windows and natural light. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Brian Deese, a bald man in a dark suit and red tie, speaking intensely about being "well within the consensus of the experts" regarding economic predictions. The scene cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blazer over an open-collared shirt, listening intently before asking if there was "any regret about not taking the foot off the pedal" concerning federal government actions. Deese responds, "absolutely." The video then transitions to black-and-white archival footage of a man in a suit seated in a formal room with a fireplace and ornate decor, engaged in conversation with an older man holding his glasses, evoking a presidential or high-level political meeting. The footage returns to the present, where James Jacoby, identified by a lower-third as Correspondent for FRONTLINE, identifies Deese as "one of the chief architects of the Biden Administration's $1.9 trillion dollar rescue plan" and asks if inflation concerns were part of their internal deliberation for the "Rescue Act." Brian Deese, now identified by a lower-third as 'Dir., National Economic Council, 2021-23,' acknowledges that it was an issue they were "always aware of" and a "trade-off" in policymaking "in the face of uncertainty." Jacoby clarifies, "You're saying that you knew that that could be a potential trade-off." Deese confirms it was "always a trade-off," explaining the logic behind their actions was "to get ahead of the pandemic, help bridge for families and businesses, and also ensure against the downside risks to our economy." The atmosphere is serious and professional, with the FRONTLINE logo visible, as the camera alternates between Deese and Jacoby, who occasionally gestures with a pen.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, with dark hair and a beard, speaking earnestly in a dimly lit, wood-paneled room. His clear, formal voice discusses how the US economy's inflation challenge is not unique, being a global issue with higher inflation in Europe and the UK. His facial expressions shift from serious to slightly smiling as he speaks. The camera cuts to a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, who listens intently and then, with a thoughtful, slightly concerned expression, asks in a clear voice if there was concern at the White House about the Fed running the economy "too hot for too long." The man in the suit firmly responds that he will "institutionally not answer" that question, prompting the bald man to ask "Why?" The man in the suit then explains that the independence of monetary policy-making is a hallmark of their system, which this administration is committed to, and therefore they make deliberate choices not to comment on such questions. The scene then transitions abruptly to an exterior night shot of a grand classical building, likely the New York Stock Exchange, adorned with a massive American flag draped across its facade, dramatically illuminated by streetlights. Smaller American flags wave on flagpoles below, and the classical architecture is visible under the dark sky, evoking a sense of national significance and financial gravity. As this shot appears, background music begins, and a new female voice introduces a segment about "the rough road to recovery."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, low-angle shot of the New York Stock Exchange at night, its illuminated classical facade draped with an American flag, as a narrator discusses 'America's economy' and how 'stimulus from the Fed and the government would contribute to a rapid economic recovery' through late 2021 and into 2022. President Joe Biden then appears at a podium in a formal chamber, flanked by flags and the presidential seal, stating, 'As our economy has come roaring back, we see some price increases.' The scene transitions to a bustling supermarket with masked shoppers navigating crowded aisles, visually representing the impact as a narrator explains that 'inflation continued climbing at the fastest pace in decades, hitting the poor and middle class the hardest,' followed by another voice emphasizing, 'these price increases will be real impact on families, they're not going away anytime soon.' A title card reads 'CHAPTER FOURTEEN: A Different World' before an aerial view of a busy highway in a desert-like urban area, accompanied by the sound of a car engine starting and driving. A speaker declares, 'This is the epicenter of this rise in inflation,' with a female voice adding, 'the highest inflation rate of any major city in the country,' and a narrator specifying, 'No city had it worse than Phoenix, which had the highest inflation rate in the nation.' The visual shifts to a drive-through aid distribution, where volunteers in gloves hand out supplies to people in cars, with the sound of a car driving and a 'thud' as items are exchanged. A speaker describes visiting 'St. Mary's Food Bank' where 'the cars were lined up first thing in the morning,' and his team receives daily emails about the number of people served, as the final shot shows a long line of vehicles waiting, underscoring widespread need.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a bustling drive-through food distribution event under a clear sky, showing cars lined up under white tents and orange traffic cones guiding the flow. Volunteers in high-visibility vests are actively loading boxes into vehicles, including a white pickup truck and a silver minivan, as recipients wait in their cars. Tom Kertis, President and CEO of St. Mary’s Food Bank, identified by a FRONTLINE lower-third, speaks directly to the camera in a blue shirt, explaining that they served "1007 households" yesterday, not just people, feeding "four, five people" per household, five days a week, because people "just don't have any other choice" due to inflation eating up their budgets, forcing them to choose between food and other necessities. The scene then shifts to a group of volunteers in yellow and orange vests efficiently unloading and distributing food from carts to cars under the tents. Two women, one in a black t-shirt with a visible tattoo and the other in a patterned tank top with glasses, walk and converse animatedly; one of them shares her struggle as a single mom, stating that "life has gotten a lot more expensive" and it's like "choosing between your rent and your food," recounting how "yesterday I spent 100 bucks just to get cereal, milk, and bread, and eggs" for a week's worth of food that "is not gonna feed six kids." The video returns to the broader scene, showing a long line of cars moving slowly through the distribution area, with industrial buildings and a diesel tanker truck in the background. An unseen interviewer then asks, "When did you start seeing an increase in people coming?", to which Tom Kertis responds, "It was the end of February this year, 2022." The final shot focuses on a black Ford pickup truck approaching the distribution point, with a yellow traffic barrier in the foreground, underscoring the organized, high-volume nature of the operation amidst the ongoing demand.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a bustling drive-through food distribution event under a large white tent, where vehicles like a silver minivan and a black pickup truck are queued by orange cones. Volunteers in high-visibility vests efficiently load boxes, including those labeled 'St. Mary's Food Bank' and 'CSFP', into cars. A volunteer in a yellow vest is seen pushing a cart filled with these boxes, which bear the 'St. Mary's Food Bank Alliance' logo. The scene transitions to an interview with Tom Kertis, President and CEO of St. Mary's Food Bank, wearing a blue button-up shirt with the organization's logo. With a serious yet approachable expression, he explains, "We saw a slight uptick... it kept climbing... We've seen a 26% increase year over year in the number of people coming to us for help," noting that "18% of the people are first-time people coming to the food bank." He adds that the situation is "worse now" than during the pandemic because "food was more available during the pandemic. We're seeing food availability going down," and predicts it's "probably going to get worse before it's going to get better, unfortunately." A continuous low hum underscores his speech. The camera briefly shows another man in a dark shirt listening intently, before returning to wide shots of the ongoing distribution. Later, a man with long gray hair and glasses, receiving food, responds to an off-screen question, stating he's "just trying to get a little extra food" and "trying to stretch the dollar." A distinct, low-frequency sound design or music plays during this segment, emphasizing the gravity of the situation as vehicles continue to move through the well-coordinated distribution point under clear skies.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with close-ups of two men engaged in conversation outdoors, likely in a parking lot with vehicles and orange traffic cones. A man with long gray hair and glasses, his hair blowing in the wind, speaks about the difficulty of feeding two people for a week on a $200 grocery budget, noting he's been coming to this place for about six months and is working but it's 'just not enough.' A bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue button-up, asks if he's been coming a long time and if he's working. The scene then transitions to a motorcyclist in a black helmet and jacket, riding through a cone-marked parking lot, suggesting a delivery or training route, accompanied by a brief musical interlude. A narrator's voice then states that similar stories were heard from credit counselors and clients at a money management counseling center. The setting shifts to a modern office building with palm trees, where a group of diverse individuals, including a woman in a red jacket and a man in a dark shirt, are seated in a bright, minimalist conference room for what appears to be a counseling or support session. Kate Bulger, Director of Research at Money Management Intl., with shoulder-length hair and glasses, speaks with expressive hand gestures about clients who, for the first time, can't pay their bills, often stating, 'I'm a good person. I've always paid my bills before.' Craig Bleck, a bald man with a beard and a counselor at Money Management Intl., is seen speaking and gesturing, while a man in a white striped shirt with graying hair adds that inflation has 'eaten away their savings,' and emergency funds are 'gone.' Craig Bleck then asks if they are seeing new clients rather than just those with chronic credit problems. The atmosphere is serious and focused, with natural light streaming through large windows, and participants in business casual attire, highlighting a professional yet supportive environment as the camera alternates between close-ups and wider shots of the group discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man with short dark hair, a beard, and glasses, wearing a dark plaid shirt, listening intently in a bright, minimalist conference room, as a male voice is heard discussing the influx of 'new people' coming in. The man in the dark plaid shirt then asks, 'How real are rent increases right now?' The scene widens to reveal a group seated in a circle of black office chairs, engaged in a discussion, with natural light filling the modern, open office space. The camera pans across the group, showing a man with white hair and a beard in a brown shirt, another man in a red shirt, and a woman in a black dress with glasses, identified as DOMINIQUE PAYTON, Client. The focus then moves to WANDA JENKINS, Counselor, Money Management Intl., a woman in a red jacket over a black dress, who speaks passionately with expressive hand gestures. She describes an 'uptick of new families and individuals' at a local shelter in Phoenix who can no longer afford their living situations due to rent increases of '$500 to a thousand dollars in one month.' Wanda continues, empathizing with clients who call her 'angry,' needing help but also expressing 'shame' and 'crying,' stating, 'I was one of them.' The group appears to be in a supportive, structured session, likely a financial counseling or therapy group, maintaining a calm, focused atmosphere.

audio_updated_entities.Wanda Jenkins: A woman with short curly hair, wearing a red jacket over a black dress and large hoop earrings, identified as 'Counselor, Money Management Intl.', who speaks passionately about an uptick in families unable to afford housing due to rent increases of $500-$1000 in one month, and describes clients calling in anger, shame, and tears, acknowledging she was once in their situation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated in a modern, well-lit office environment, holding a pen and papers, gesturing expressively as he asks, "Are your numbers up in terms of people that are seeking out help at the moment?" The camera then cuts to a woman with shoulder-length brown hair, wearing blue-framed glasses, a black top, and a gold crescent moon necklace, who gestures animatedly as she responds, explaining that "it's not just that we're getting more calls, it's that the folks who are calling us are in greater distress," detailing how people are now behind on credit cards, utilities, and housing payments, facing "greater economic challenges" than a few years ago. The scene shifts to John Adel, identified by a lower-third as a 'Client,' wearing a dark blue polo shirt, who speaks with a concerned expression, his hands moving slightly, stating, "It's a different world, but I have to tell you, I go to the store and I am just shocked," adding that he's "keeping my nose above the waves right now, but I feel like that wave is a lot bigger than I thought and it's behind me and it's coming." This dialogue unfolds in what appears to be a group therapy or financial counseling session, with participants seated in a circle in a bright, minimalist room with large windows. A dramatic, slightly ominous musical score begins as the segment transitions to a black screen with white text reading 'CHAPTER FIFTEEN: Things Are Gonna Get Harder,' followed by a dramatic sunset over the Washington Monument and U.S. Capitol, with the PBS and FRONTLINE logos appearing. The final shot shows the exterior of the U.S. Treasury Building under a clear blue sky, with American flags flying and pedestrians walking by, as an unseen narrator states, "In the fall of 2021, with inflation at 6.8%, well above the Fed's 2% target, Chairman Powell acknowledged it might not be transitory after all."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of the Federal Reserve Building under a clear blue sky, establishing a formal setting. Inside a hearing room, Jerome Powell, identified by his nameplate 'THE HONORABLE JEROME H. POWELL,' is seen in a dark suit, white shirt, and blue striped tie, speaking into a microphone and gesturing with his right hand. His voice is heard discussing the term "transitory," explaining that while many interpret it as short-lived, the Federal Reserve uses it to mean something that won't leave a permanent mark in the form of higher inflation, suggesting it's time to retire the word for clearer communication. A black-and-white close-up emphasizes his serious expression before a camera shutter click accompanies a transition to a press briefing from May 2022. Here, Powell stands at a podium with the American flag and Federal Reserve seal behind him. A narrator states that this marks a new phase in the "easy money experiment," with the committee determined to restore price stability. Powell then thanks attendees and invites questions. As the scene shifts to a room full of journalists and reporters, many using laptops, the narrator explains that interest rates would be raised over several months. Powell's voice returns, greeting attendees and expressing pleasure at seeing everyone in person for the first time in years. The scene cuts to a CNBC news segment featuring a male anchor in a dark suit and teal tie, with a 'SQUAWK BOX' graphic asking 'HOW HIGH WILL THE FUNDS RATE GO?'. The narrator continues, detailing that in response to rising inflation, the Fed would pause quantitative easing and begin tightening. Powell's voice is heard again, announcing that at "today's meeting," the committee raised the target range for the federal funds rate and decided to begin reducing the balance sheet. The narrator concludes by noting that neither Powell nor other Fed officials have precisely explained the extent of the Fed's actions, maintaining a formal, high-stakes tone throughout the discussion of Federal Reserve policy decisions.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a glitchy, distorted image of a financial news anchor (Woman in Black Blazer) on CNBC, as an unseen male voice reports on the Federal Reserve raising a key interest rate by three-quarters of a percent, its biggest hike in nearly three decades, accompanied by a clear graphic illustrating the rate increase. This transitions to a CNN news broadcast where a Male News Anchor and a Woman in Striped Top discuss the 'HISTORIC HIKE' in interest rates, with graphics of money and a 'FOR SALE' sign, while another unseen male voice confirms the Fed has raised rates again, a move previously deemed unfathomable. The scene then shifts to a dashcam view of a car driving through a war-torn urban area with smoke rising and a helicopter over a burning town, as unseen voices discuss "the war in Ukraine" and Russia "picking off Ukraine's military facilities." Next, a street in China under lockdown is shown with people in protective suits and police cars, a news ticker reporting 'CITIES UNDER LOCKDOWN' due to new COVID-19 cases, while an unseen voice announces "Lockdowns in China" and another details Shenzhen's city-wide lockdown. A man is then seen pumping gas at a station, with a price display showing $90.22 for 12.890 gallons, highlighting inflation, as an unseen voice notes "companies raising prices" and a distinct male voice adds, "A little bit of inflation is always good in our business." The video transitions to a serene aerial view of a lush green valley, followed by a man (Man Walking in Garden), identified by audio as Jerome Powell, in a suit walking through a garden, smiling, then in a black-and-white shot with a serious expression. Throughout these latter scenes, multiple unseen male voices, over a continuous background score, discuss how these events "would all send inflation even higher and accelerate the Fed's moves," referencing "Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell speaking at an annual economic summit in Jackson Hole, Wyoming," and his signal to "keep the Fed on course," despite a past call that "didn't age well," alluding to the ongoing efforts of "raising rates."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Jerome Powell, in a dark suit, white shirt, and blue striped tie, standing seriously at a wooden podium against a wood-paneled backdrop, reading from notes. An unseen male voice sets the context, stating that while higher interest rates, slower growth, and softer labor market conditions will bring down inflation, they will also bring "some pain to households and businesses." The scene then cuts to a close-up of Neel Kashkari, President & CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, bald and wearing a dark suit with a red tie, speaking expressively with wide eyes in a modern, well-lit room with large windows, identified by a 'FRONTLINE' lower-third. Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a short beard in a dark blue button-up shirt, is shown listening intently, holding a pen to his lips. Jacoby questions Kashkari, asking how higher interest rates will help people struggling with rising gas, food, and rent bills. Kashkari responds, explaining that high prices are due to too much demand, and raising interest rates will slow down housing demand, preventing home prices and rents from climbing, which "should benefit workers." He clarifies that gas prices are not driven by the Fed but by "the war, Russia invading Ukraine, Saudi Arabia cutting back production, big geopolitical forces." The professional setting and natural light emphasize the gravity of this economic discussion, shifting from a formal address to an intimate interview.

segments.integrated_caption: In a modern, well-lit office with large windows, Correspondent James Jacoby, bald with a graying beard and wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, listens intently, occasionally holding a pen to his lips, as he discusses the limitations of direct action on certain issues. He then challenges Neel Kashkari, President and CEO of the Fed. Reserve Bank of Minneapolis, asking if economic problems can be solved with a 'scalpel' or if a 'hammer' is needed, referencing the idea that interest rates are like a sledgehammer. Kashkari, bald and dressed in a dark suit with a red tie, responds with intense, expressive gestures and wide eyes, explaining that while he would have preferred a 'scalpel' and initially believed many factors were transitory, the situation evolved, necessitating the 'hammer' to prevent things from getting 'out of control.' Jacoby, maintaining a focused, contemplative demeanor, then presses Kashkari further, asking what he says to critics who claim the Fed ran things 'too hot for too long,' calling it an 'epic mistake.' Kashkari, continuing his animated explanation, counters by pointing to other central banks that adjusted more quickly but are still facing 'very similar inflation,' acknowledging the benefit of hindsight. The tense, high-stakes conversation is underscored by the speakers' serious facial expressions and dynamic interaction.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment features a tense, close-up conversation between two men in a modern, well-lit office environment with large windows and soft natural light. The first man, bald with a graying beard, wears a dark blue button-up shirt and listens intently, occasionally gesturing with a pen held to his lips, suggesting deep thought or concern. The second man, also bald, is dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, and speaks with intense, expressive gestures and wide eyes, indicating urgency or emphasis in his dialogue. The camera alternates between tight close-ups of each man, capturing subtle facial expressions—furrowed brows, raised eyebrows, and open mouths—as they engage in what appears to be a serious, high-stakes discussion. The background remains blurred but suggests a professional setting with neutral tones and modern furnishings, including a visible lamp and window frames. The interaction evolves from listening to animated speaking, with the second man using hand gestures to underscore his points, while the first man maintains a focused, contemplative demeanor, occasionally nodding or responding with minimal movement. The atmosphere is charged with gravity, suggesting a critical business or policy-related exchange.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a bald man in a dark suit and red tie, his serious, focused expression setting a high-stakes tone. The scene then transitions to an exterior shot of a house, revealing two men through a window, seated at a table in a sunlit, modern sunroom. The 'Man in Light Blue Shirt,' wearing a light blue shirt and striped tie, is animatedly speaking and gesturing, heard stating, 'wish we had tightened sooner, but I'm not kidding myself to think it would have made a big difference in where we are in inflation today.' Across from him, the 'Man in Dark Blue Shirt,' bald with a short beard and in a dark blue button-up, listens intently with a pen to his lips, his concentration emphasized in a close-up. The focus shifts to a 'Laptop Screen' displaying a CNBC live broadcast of 'Jerome Powell,' identified as 'Chair, Federal Reserve,' speaking at a podium with the American flag and Federal Reserve seal behind him. A CNBC narrator's voice provides context, mentioning 'business reporter Chris Leonard' and that the Fed was 'Moving at the fastest pace in 40 years,' before Powell is heard saying 'Good afternoon' and beginning to speak about the FOMC. The narrator then summarizes that the FOMC 'raised its policy interest rate by three quarters of a percentage point.' The video alternates between Powell's live feed and the two men, capturing their reactions. The 'Man in Light Blue Shirt' appears increasingly concerned and expressive as he discusses the implications, explaining, 'It can be a little bit hard to understand because you hear, okay, the Fed hiked rates today to 3 and a half percent. Well, what does that mean? That my credit card rate is going to be a little bit higher or I'll have to borrow more money for a house.' He continues, emphasizing, 'He is talking about a fundamental restructuring of the financial system. The the financial system globally has been built around extremely low, ultra-low interest rates for 10 years.' Powell is then heard again, stating, 'My colleagues and I are strongly committed to bringing inflation back down to our 2% goal.' The 'Man in Light Blue Shirt' concludes, 'I think people don't appreciate the magnitude of what the Fed did.' The bright, airy sunroom contrasts with the gravity of the financial news, as the sequence ends with a return to the CNBC screen, reinforcing the importance of Powell’s statement while the two men continue their intense conversation, underscoring the real-world impact of the Federal Reserve’s decision.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a sunlit room with large windows, where a man in a light blue shirt and striped tie animatedly explains a point, gesturing with both hands, his voice discussing how the economy is facing a "long-term thing playing out over time, probably over like a year or two," shifting to a "higher rate environment" and the "correction that that's going to cause." He emphasizes that "things aren't going to get easier. Things are going to get harder." Across from him, a bald man in a dark blue button-up shirt listens intently, pen to his lips. The scene abruptly shifts to a nighttime urban setting, showing silhouettes of people walking on a sidewalk with sharp shadows, then panning to a bustling city street with a yellow taxi and steam rising from the pavement. The audio transitions to a broadcast voice declaring, "Tonight, the economic alarms are blaring," followed by another voice warning of "economic upheaval" and "heightened concerns about a recession," and a third stating the Fed's "number one priority is fighting inflation even if it means the jobless rate, unemployment, goes up." The visuals then show a diverse crowd gathered outside a 'Wall Street Station' subway station, holding signs like 'WE SUPPORT UNION EFFORTS' and 'STARBUCKS UNITED'. A male voice from the rally passionately asks, "Are we gonna let this corporation stop workers from joining our union?" to which the crowd loudly responds, "NO!" The charged atmosphere of activism is illuminated by streetlights as a woman in a red jacket, identified by a broadcast voice as Liz Shuler, steps forward to speak into a microphone, holding a 'WE SUPPORT STARBUCKS WORKERS' sign, stating, "I'm here with 12 and a half million union members." The broadcast voice then adds that Liz Shuler leads the largest labor organization in the country.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a charged nighttime outdoor protest scene, where a crowd gathers on steps and sidewalks, holding signs. A female voice from the crowd passionately shouts, "Listen to your workers!", followed by loud cheers and applause, underscoring the urgency of the public demonstration. The scene then transitions to a brightly lit indoor interview setting. Liz Shuler, President of the AFL-CIO, with shoulder-length blonde hair and wearing a gray blazer over a blue top, speaks earnestly, gesturing slightly. She explains that she met with Chairman Powell and the Board of Governors because the Fed "doesn't often get to hear from actual working people and and how they're seeing things in the economy." James Jacoby, Correspondent for FRONTLINE, bald with a short beard and wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, listens intently, holding a pen to his lips. He then asks Shuler about her message to the Fed regarding raising interest rates. Shuler responds emphatically that "raising the interest rates is bad for working people," arguing it puts the post-pandemic economic trajectory at risk. She elaborates that in a consumer-driven economy, if working people cannot make ends meet, they won't buy goods, which will "grind the economy to a halt." She concludes that aggressive moves should not "throw people out of work and basically balance the economy on the backs of working people." Jacoby interjects, noting that "the Fed is tasked with controlling inflation and it and and inflation is definitely..." as the segment ends, with the red FRONTLINE logo visible in the lower third.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a male voice asking why one would advocate for the Fed to take its foot off the pedal. Immediately following, a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair, wearing a gray blazer over a blue top, is seen in a close-up, speaking with a serious expression and occasional hand gestures in a softly lit indoor setting with dark curtains and teal vases. She explains that the interest rate hikes were happening too quickly and wouldn't impact gas and food prices, which are major concerns for working people. The scene then cuts to Dion Rabouin, a Black man with braided hair, wearing rectangular glasses and a light blue dress shirt, identified by a lower-third from The Wall Street Journal. Speaking directly to the camera with animated facial expressions and hand gestures in a softly lit room, he asserts that the Fed's goal is to make businesses not want to hire people or to lay them off, estimating that the unemployment rate would rise to 4.5% by year-end, implying millions would lose their jobs. Following this, a bald man in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie appears in a modern, well-lit room with large windows, speaking with expressive hand gestures and wide eyes, acknowledging the best-case scenario of bringing down inflation. The final shot shows a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, seated across from another man, listening intently with a pen to his lips, his expression serious and focused, in a modern interior with large windows and natural light.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, holding a pen near his mouth in deep thought. He gestures as he speaks, questioning if avoiding a recession while employment stays strong and inflation remains high would necessitate hurting the jobs market. The scene then cuts to another bald man, identified by a lower-third as 'Pres. & CEO, Fed. Reserve Bank of Minneapolis', dressed in a dark suit with a white shirt and red tie. With wide eyes and expressive hand movements, he responds with urgency, stating that they must keep raising rates until inflation is down, but expresses mild optimism that a recovery could be very quick if a recession were to occur. The first man then asks how remote the possibility of much higher unemployment is in the coming years, to which the Fed CEO replies that it's not remote and hard to put odds on. The intense discussion, set in a modern, well-lit interior with large windows, concludes with a distinct "whoosh" sound as the scene transitions to a black screen displaying 'CHAPTER SIXTEEN' and 'The Tide Goes Out,' signaling a narrative shift.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a tense, investigative background music as a wide shot of a modern, open-plan office with employees at desks under bright fluorescent lighting transitions to a bustling factory floor where workers in safety gear operate machinery and a forklift moves pallets. An aerial view of a massive warehouse with towering racks is followed by a nighttime exterior of a glass skyscraper with illuminated windows, while a male narrator states that "Throughout 2022, the economy remained strong," with "unemployment hovered near historic lows," echoed by a female news anchor reporting "unemployment at a half-century low." The narrator adds that "Wages were on the rise," with another male anchor confirming "about 5% annually," leading the Fed to "continue pumping the brakes to try to cool down inflation." A female anchor explains that "The Fed has been raising rates in hopes of slowing the economy," but "the economy isn't really slowing down quickly." The scene then cuts to a car assembly line with robotic arms and workers, before shifting to the New York Stock Exchange trading floor, where the narrator declares, "That riled Wall Street." Traders in blue jackets and headsets move quickly amid monitors displaying real-time stock data, as a male news anchor warns of "the growing possibility of a recession," with "Wall Street spent another day in turmoil." A female anchor adds, "And you're probably feeling it in those 401Ks. Stocks are..." The narrator continues, "For the stock market and bond market, it was the worst year since the Great Financial Crisis in 2008," while the female anchor notes, "The Nasdaq down for four straight quarters for the first time since the dot-com bust." The camera focuses on individual traders, including one in a blue vest with glasses intensely monitoring multiple screens, and another writing notes. The segment concludes with a close-up interview of Mohamed A. El-Erian, Chief Economic Advisor at Allianz, speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting with a blurred background, identified by a lower-third graphic, as he states, "In 2022, we've had this very unusual situation whereby you've made double-digit losses," with the FRONTLINE logo appearing in the corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Mohamed El-Erian, an older man with gray hair and a mustache, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, speaking with a serious and engaged expression in a professional indoor setting, explaining that "on both risky assets, stocks, and risk-free assets, US Treasuries... there's been absolutely nowhere to hide," highlighting it as a "big issue for retirement plans, pension systems." He emphasizes that "no matter how well you diversified your portfolio, there was no risk mitigation in it at all." The camera then cuts to a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, listening intently with a pen held to his lips, who interjects, "It was all losses," a sentiment echoed by El-Erian. A light, investigative background music begins as a female voiceover states, "US existing home sales plunged to a 12-year low in December." The scene transitions to an aerial view of a suburban neighborhood, showcasing rows of houses with varied roof colors and well-maintained lawns, with the camera panning across the grid-like streets and curving roads, while the female voiceover continues, "The Federal Reserve's interest rate hiking cycle has pushed down..." The video then cuts to Dion Rabouin, a Black man with braided hair, wearing rectangular glasses, a light blue dress shirt, and a patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a softly lit room, elaborating that "housing prices have started actually coming down for the first time in a very long time." The female voiceover adds, "Mortgage applications have decreased," before Rabouin continues, "The crypto market, you've seen a number of companies wiped out." A Bloomberg news graphic appears, displaying a Bitcoin price chart with a downward trend from January to July 2022, and the headline 'BITCOIN DEVOTEES PONDER A MARKET BOTTOM,' as the female voiceover declares, "Crypto winter is here." Rabouin concludes by stating, "That is not unrelated to what's happened with the Fed." The final scene shifts to a chaotic, handheld shot of a crowd of people, including journalists and onlookers, surrounding a man in a suit, with cameras and phones raised, capturing a moment of intense public attention and media frenzy, accompanied by shouts of "hold on a second, let's go, let's go," indistinct voices, and camera clicks, while the female voiceover mentions, "Sam Bankman-Fried faces investigations..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the chaotic sounds of a media scrum, featuring indistinct voices and camera clicks, as a man in a suit is swarmed by reporters. This frenetic energy quickly shifts to a visual of a social media post from @ksicrypto, displaying a tweet about someone who 'bought the dip and lost 😂,' accompanied by screenshots of a crypto wallet plummeting from over $2.9 million to just over $1,000. A male voice narrates this dramatic loss, stating, 'He invested three million dollars into Luna. And now it's worth a thousand.' The scene then transitions to a calm, well-lit interview setting, where Steven Pearlstein, identified by a FRONTLINE lower-third as a contributing columnist for The Washington Post, speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands. His articulate voice explains how 'you create these asset bubbles,' detailing 'bubbles in stock markets and bond markets and real estate markets and art markets,' and how 'those bubbles burst and then that causes a downturn.' A narrator briefly introduces Pearlstein, noting his nearly 40 years of reporting on financial markets. Pearlstein continues, explaining that 'bubbles tend to be everything bubbles these days' because 'if the source of it is cheap money, then you can be pretty much sure that it's not just real estate or it's not just stocks or it's not just tech and telecom... it's not just Bitcoin.' He emphasizes that 'these things are connected by rubber bands with each other' and 'what propels one propels all of them.' Throughout Pearlstein's discussion, the interviewer, a bald man in a dark blue shirt, listens intently, occasionally holding a pen to his lips, reflecting the serious tone of the expert analysis.

The video then cuts to a calm, well-lit interview setting where Steven Pearlstein, identified as a contributing columnist for The Washington Post, speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands as he discusses financial matters. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with grainy archival footage of two older men in suits at a formal event, as a male voice introduces a famous line by investor Warren Buffett: "you don't find out who's been swimming naked until the tide goes out." This is followed by several different male voices repeating variations of the quote, emphasizing the idea of exposure when economic conditions change. The scene then transitions to a modern interview setting where Rana Foroohar, Associate Editor at the Financial Times, is seen in a light blue turtleneck, speaking expressively. She echoes the sentiment, stating, "When interest rates start to rise and the tide pulls out as Warren Buffett would say." The camera cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby for FRONTLINE, a bald man with a short beard, listening intently with a pen to his lips. Foroohar continues, explaining that many, including herself, anticipate a "bigger correction at some point." Jacoby, seen in a modern interior with large windows, then asks, "But look, when you when you say you expect there to be a bigger correction, what does that actually mean? I mean, a lot of people are going to see this and get concerned about that obviously." Foroohar responds with a "Yeah, yeah," and adds, "let me let me try and be honest but not scare people," to which Jacoby interjects with a brief "Yeah." Foroohar then states, "So the markets were down 20% last year. That seems like a lot," as the video alternates between her and Jacoby, maintaining a serious, professional tone throughout their economic discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman with dark, shoulder-length hair, wearing a light blue turtleneck sweater and small gold hoop earrings. Speaking with expressive facial movements and a clear, analytical voice, she discusses market cycles, stating that in a normal cycle, they'd be at the bottom, but due to the 'age of easy money' and decades of declining rates and increasing debt, she cannot safely declare they've hit bottom. She emphasizes that assets have arguably been artificially inflated and questions the possibility of a continued correction, her eyes shifting slightly as she articulates these serious financial insights with subtle shifts in expression. Around the 35-second mark, the camera cuts to a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, who listens intently, his gaze fixed on the woman, holding a pen to his lips in deep concentration within a modern, well-lit interior. The camera then cuts back to the woman, who continues, asserting her belief that they are in a 'once in a lifetime financial transition' and advises everyone to 'strap in,' cautioning those who need their money in the next couple of years to be more prudent. The soft, natural lighting throughout creates a calm, focused atmosphere for this serious discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping low-angle shot of the New York Stock Exchange at dusk, its grand neoclassical facade illuminated and adorned with a massive American flag draped across the front, flanked by smaller flags on poles. The camera tilts upward, capturing the towering skyscrapers surrounding the financial district, as the PBS logo appears in the lower left corner, followed by the FRONTLINE logo. A male narrator begins, stating that "In these early months of 2023, on Wall Street, some have been betting that the Fed will relent, stop raising interest rates as inflation cools down," adding that "the higher it pushes rates, and the longer it does, the greater the risks." The scene cuts to Mohamed A. El-Erian, a man in a dark suit and teal tie, speaking with a serious, thoughtful expression in a softly lit indoor setting. He explains, "And so the marketplace is saying the Fed is gonna go too far and it's gonna be forced to reverse course. That is really unusual. We've got to a situation now where the markets dismiss what the Fed is telling us. It's a moment where there are many more potential outcomes. Some are fine, a soft landing, some are not, a hard landing, and the truth is, you cannot distinguish enough between them." The camera returns to the NYSE, showing the flag billowing in the wind under streetlights, before cutting back to El-Erian, who gestures slightly as he speaks. The narrator then introduces "one of the most pessimistic voices," economist Nouriel Roubini, who became famous for his accurate prediction of the financial crisis in 2008. The video transitions to a close-up of Roubini, wearing thick-rimmed glasses and a dark suit, speaking with a grave, intense expression in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, the FRONTLINE logo still visible. He begins to speak with measured, deliberate movements, stating, "We have had literally a few decades of uh every..." his face etched with concern, underscoring the gravity of the economic discussion against the iconic backdrop of the financial world.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Nouriel Roubini, an economist with glasses, wearing a dark suit and white shirt, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls. His intense expression and slight gestures accompany his emphatic speech about "increasing bubbles" that have been "fed and supported by central banks," fueled by "excessive leverage, excessive private and public borrowing, and excessive risk-taking." He declares, "The party is over," as inflation is high and rising, central banks increase interest rates, bursting asset bubbles and increasing debt servicing, leading to an "economic and a financial nightmare." The camera occasionally cuts to a listener before returning to him. The scene then transitions to a wide shot of a man in a dark suit walking down a luxurious hallway with golden walls, passing a reception desk with a hat on it, and approaching an elevator, with the embossed name 'GUGGENHEIM' visible on the wall. The next scene shifts to a modern office with large windows, where an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and blue shirt, is seated across from a bald man in a dark shirt. They shake hands and engage in a conversation, with the older man listening intently and nodding. A narrator's voice explains that if Roubini's prediction of a debt crisis is correct, then Jim Millstein (the older man) would be on the front lines, known on Wall Street as the expert countries and companies turn to for debt restructuring. The camera focuses on the older man’s face, capturing his thoughtful expression as he speaks, with reflections of the cityscape visible in his glasses, while the bald man is heard briefly asking about a "debt crisis." The atmosphere is professional and serious, suggesting a high-stakes business or financial discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man in a dark blue button-up shirt, holding a pen thoughtfully to his lips, listening intently. He asks, "This is actually be good for your business?" The camera then cuts to Jim Millstein, Co-chairman of Guggenheim Securities, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, seated across from Jacoby in a modern, well-lit conference room. Millstein, with a serious and engaged expression, chuckles briefly and responds, "You know, I'm getting a little too old for this." Jacoby, maintaining eye contact, prompts, "Too old for what?" Millstein, occasionally turning to look out the large windows reflecting the cityscape, explains, "This business. No, I, I, uh, yeah, no, it'll be, it'll be a boom for the restructuring business. But I just don't think it's avoidable at this point. I think we're just, you know, the, the, the bill has come due. Um, and it, it's gonna have to be paid." Jacoby then asks, "How worried are you about what's happening right now?" Millstein, with a thoughtful demeanor, states, "I've never been more worried in the 42 years that I've been a professional, either as a lawyer, banker, or government, uh, servant. The American corporate sector has never been more levered in American history. Never had more debt. And American households are just about as, um, levered as they were, um, heading into the 2008." The FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower left corner throughout this intense discussion, set against the professional, minimalistic backdrop of the conference room.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of Jim Millstein, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit over a light blue shirt, seated in a modern conference room. He speaks with a serious and engaged expression, detailing how the 2008 financial crisis involved various loans—student, mortgage, car, personal, and credit card—and asserts that "we've borrowed a lot of money as a people." He continues, stating the Fed is right to raise interest rates to slow economic activity, but warns that "the most highly leveraged players in our economy are gonna come under real stress," whether households, businesses, or governments, as interest costs rise. The camera then cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby, a bald man with a short beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, who listens intently, holding a pen to his lips. Jacoby then gestures with his hand while speaking, asking if they should be preparing for "a bursting of this massive credit bubble." Millstein, with a focused demeanor, responds, "It's happening right in front of us... It's happening right now." Jacoby, still gesturing, lightens the tone by asking, "Are you usually this gloomy or am I just getting you on a bad day?" to which Millstein replies with a slight chuckle, "Got me on a bad day." Jacoby then adds, "One of the concerns is that there's a kind of debt bomb out there." The scene briefly shifts to Neel Kashkari, a bald man in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, speaking with expressive hand gestures and wide eyes, indicating a passionate or urgent tone, though his voice is not heard in this segment. The professional, well-lit setting with large windows remains consistent, underscoring the serious financial discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in October 2022 with Neel Kashkari, a bald man in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie, speaking intensely with wide eyes and expressive hand gestures from a modern office with a blurred cityscape outside. He states, "We're looking at the data. We're not seeing evidence of such a popping. We're not seeing evidence of delinquencies taking off... Households on average have very strong balance sheets. The big banks... are well capitalized relative to where they were before 2008. So we're not seeing evidence of it yet." The camera cuts to a bald man with a short beard in a dark blue button-up shirt, listening seriously, who then asks, "How concerned are you about a credit bubble popping?" and later, "how much disruption in the financial markets are you willing to tolerate now that they're adjusting to this new interest rate environment after more than a decade of of zero rates." Kashkari, with continued passion and hand gestures, responds, "We live in a market economy. And market participants need to find a way to adjust to a changing economic landscape. It's not the Fed's job to bail out Wall Street investors if their stock portfolios go down. Obviously, we need to keep systemic risk from spilling across the whole economy..." The tense, professional atmosphere highlights their focused discussion on economic and financial matters.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Neel Kashkari, bald and wearing a dark suit with a red tie, speaking intensely in a modern office with large windows, his expression urgent as he gestures with his hand, stating that while they are prepared to act, the bar for the Fed's action should be "quite high." The scene cuts to Correspondent James Jacoby, bald with a short beard and wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, listening intently with a pen to his lips, then gesturing as he speaks, questioning how much "disruption" would be needed for the Fed to intervene, given past rescues of "vulnerable and brittle" financial markets. Kashkari responds that they are "a long, long way away" from such a disruption. A narrator then interjects, stating that less than five months after that interview, the Fed would indeed have to step in. The setting shifts to black-and-white footage outside Silicon Valley Bank (SVB), where crowds gather, a Brinks armored truck is parked, and a police officer in uniform gestures to manage the crowd, while camera crews film the scene. A news anchor from Sky News Australia reports with a 'BREAKING NEWS' graphic, announcing that the "US Federal Reserve has bailed out the Silicon Valley Bank, which had collapsed over the weekend." The narrator adds that emergency measures were enacted to shore up the banking system after two banks collapsed. The video then transitions to President Joe Biden at a podium, flanked by American flags and the presidential seal, speaking formally in a government chamber, declaring this "the biggest bank collapse since the 2008 financial crisis." The sequence ends with a close-up of Biden, as the narrator raises "important questions of how these banks got into the circumstance in the first place."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with President Joe Biden speaking at a podium in a formal chamber, flanked by American flags and the presidential seal, gesturing as he addresses the audience. The scene transitions to an interview with Sheila Bair, identified as 'Chair, FDIC, 2006-11', speaking expressively in a softly lit room, stating, "We're seeing a potential fragility in the system related to monetary policy. If we hadn't been driving our economy for 14 years with easy money and then trying to really quickly undo that, no, we wouldn't be having these problems now. Absolutely not." An interviewer's voice then asks, "What should the Fed do?" to which Bair responds, "So, uh, for a long time, I have advocated that the Fed should be raising rates. But even I believe now they need to hit pause. They've gone too far, too fast. They need to hit pause and assess the impact on the financial system and the economy." During her response, the camera cuts to a bald man in a dark shirt holding a pencil to his lips in thought, then back to Bair. The sound of paper rustling is heard before the video shifts to a black-and-white shot of an older man in a suit at a table surrounded by photographers, followed by a dramatic, stylized shot of the same man under intense spotlighting with multiple camera flashes. A male narrator explains, "It's unclear what the Fed will do next, but just days before the bank failures, Jerome Powell appeared before Congress to answer tough questions about the economy." Jerome Powell is then seen, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, seated at a hearing table with papers and glasses, appearing serious and focused, stating, "And we actually don't think that we need to see a sharp or enormous increase in unemployment to get inflation under control." The video cuts to Senator Elizabeth Warren, in a purple blazer, speaking animatedly at a Senate hearing, identified by a lower-third, as she challenges Powell, asking, "I'm looking at your projections. Do you call two laying off two million people this year not a sharp increase?" A wide shot reveals the full Senate chamber with photographers and journalists. The narrator adds, "The hearing was a showcase of partisan politics and and government gridlock." The final shot focuses on Senator Kevin Cramer, identified by a lower-third, wearing a blue suit and red-striped tie, speaking with a thumbs-up gesture beside a woman in a pink blazer who smiles slightly, as he is heard saying "Chairman Brown..." and later, "raising interest rates..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark suit and red-striped tie, gesturing emphatically as he speaks into a microphone at a formal hearing, asserting that "Senate Democrats and President Biden" won't be stopped from "overtaxing, overspending." A woman in a pink blazer is visible nearby. A narrator's voice then explains that this is "yet another reminder of how in an era of political dysfunction, we've become so dependent on the Fed and on easy money to drive the American economy." The scene transitions to black-and-white footage of Jerome Powell, in a dark suit and purple tie, walking briskly down a marble hallway with 'The Federal Reserve Board of Governors' documents, followed by reporters. The video then shows Steven Pearlstein, an older man with white hair and tortoiseshell glasses, in a well-lit room with bookshelves, speaking expressively with hand gestures, stating, "The economy needs to get back into balance. And that will be painful. And if we keep putting off the day of reckoning, we'll just make the day of reckoning a bigger day of reckoning." A bald man with a beard, wearing a dark blue button-up shirt, listens intently before asking, "How do you think we'll look back at this era of easy money?" Steven Pearlstein, with a serious and thoughtful expression, responds that while it might be seen as a "golden era" due to "cheap and free money without consequences," it will ultimately be viewed as "a lesson for the future... that it was a mistake." During this exchange, a somber, ethereal instrumental background music begins. The scene shifts to a close-up of Steven Pearlstein's profile, then to serene outdoor shots of sunlight filtering through autumn leaves and a wide view of a bare tree in front of the classical Federal Reserve Building under a clear blue sky. A different male voice, with a slight accent, then adds, "I think that we're going to look back on this era as being totally..." over the continuing music.

The video then transitions to a serene outdoor shot of sunlight filtering through autumn leaves, followed by a wide shot of a bare tree in front of a classical white building with tall windows, likely the Federal Reserve Building, under a clear blue sky.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an exterior shot of the Federal Reserve Building, its classical white stone facade and prominent columns visible under a clear blue sky, with bare tree branches partially obscuring the view. A deep, sustained, slightly ominous musical drone begins to play softly in the background as the camera cuts to a close-up of Mohamed A. El-Erian, an older man with gray hair and a mustache, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, speaking with a serious and engaged expression in a professional indoor setting. In a deep, serious voice, El-Erian states that the economy didn't fulfill its potential, having lost sight of how to achieve sustainable and inclusive growth, criticizing that 'the world of easy money went way too far, way, way too far.' The scene transitions back to the Federal Reserve Building, now showing the engraved 'FEDERAL RESERVE' lettering and a sculpted eagle atop the structure, as El-Erian continues, asserting that genuine, durable, inclusive growth is needed, not artificial growth, and that none of this is in the hands of the Fed, which 'don't invest in infrastructure,' 'can't reform the tax system,' or 'help labor retraining.' The video then shifts to a panoramic view of Washington D.C., beginning with a distant shot of the U.S. Capitol Building’s dome rising above surrounding buildings, gradually zooming out to reveal the full Capitol and the Washington Monument in the hazy distance, with the cityscape stretching out under a soft, diffused light, as El-Erian concludes, 'This is a political problem.' The drone music fades out, replaced by a distinct, slightly melancholic, piano-like melody with a sustained string/synth background, followed by a brief, almost triumphant orchestral flourish, then a more sustained, hopeful, yet slightly dramatic orchestral piece with a clear melody and percussion, as the sequence ends with a black screen and a text overlay reading 'FRONTLINE is made possible by PBS Viewers Like You.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment features static, dark-themed end credits for the FRONTLINE documentary 'Age of Easy Money,' set against a blurred, abstract digital interface with faint blue light streaks, creating a technological and somber atmosphere. Throughout, the red FRONTLINE logo remains in the top left corner. White text sequentially lists production credits, including directors like James Jacoby, producers such as Anya Bourg, Sue and Edgar Wachenheim, III, Ken Druckerman, and Banks Tarver, along with editors, sound, and music personnel. Dramatic, orchestral music plays throughout the credits. A female voiceover directs viewers to "pbs.org/frontline" for more programs, as on-screen text acknowledges PBS viewers and funding foundations like the MacArthur Foundation (in blue and green text), Park Foundation (white text on a red background), Heising-Simons Foundation (with a circular logo), and the FRONTLINE Journalism Fund (in a red box). Support for the episode from 'Chasing the Dream' and 'the Hollyhock foundation' (both with picket fence graphics) is also noted, alongside archival sources including CNBC, Fox News, Getty Images, and the National Bureau of Economic Research. The female voiceover then announces that "Frontline's Age of Easy Money is available on Amazon Prime Video." Concurrently, a small black-and-white inset video appears in the bottom right, depicting a crowd of unemployed individuals, one holding a 'LAID OFF TODAY 9:30 HIRE ME' sign, with the text 'Age of Easy Money' and 'Available on prime video' displayed below it, before the orchestral music fades out.
